original_question.question: Why does geometric optics provide accurate predictions for focusing by macroscopic lenses, and why does it fail to predict diffraction patterns at small apertures?

options.A: Geometric optics works because light is a stream of particles that never diffract, so rays always follow straight lines except at interfaces.
options.B: Geometric optics works because it treats light as rays that travel in straight lines and refract at interfaces; this is a good approximation when system features are much larger than the wavelength, so diffraction is negligible, but it cannot describe diffraction or interference that arise when features are comparable to the wavelength.
options.C: Geometric optics works by solving Maxwell's equations exactly for all geometries, so there is no limitation in predicting diffraction.
options.D: Geometric optics is derived from quantum electrodynamics and therefore can describe all optical phenomena, including diffraction and interference, without approximation.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Modeling frameworks in optics — geometric optics as ray-based and physical optics as wave-based; reasons for using simplified models in practice and their limitations.
Concept 2: Wave-particle duality and quantum description — certain phenomena require quantum mechanics; photons as particles; the role of quantum optics in applying quantum theory to optical systems.

Concept 3: Electromagnetic foundation and theoretical progression — light as electromagnetic radiation; how classical EM explains many phenomena and how historical progress led from ray optics to wave optics to quantum optics.
parent_concepts: Light–matter interactions: absorption and emission spectra arise from energy-level transitions and reflect the structure and properties of matter.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why does geometric (ray) optics work well for focusing with large lenses but fail to predict diffraction at small openings?

options.A: Because it treats light as particles that never spread, so rays always go straight and never diffract.
options.B: It treats light as rays that go straight and refract at surfaces. This works when parts are much larger than the light's wavelength, but it cannot predict wave effects like diffraction.
options.C: Because geometric optics solves Maxwell's equations exactly for every shape, so it can always predict diffraction.
options.D: Because geometric optics is derived from quantum electrodynamics and thus can describe all optical effects without approximation.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened and clarified the question. Kept the core idea that ray optics is an approximation valid when features are much larger than the wavelength and cannot model wave diffraction. Options simplified to short, plausible statements.

pass_2_attempt.question: Which choice best matches the model used for each task: making eyeglasses; explaining double‑slit fringes; explaining the photoelectric effect?

options.A: Geometric (ray) optics; Physical (wave) optics; Quantum (photons) model.
options.B: Physical (wave) optics; Geometric (ray) optics; Quantum (photons) model.
options.C: Quantum (photons) model; Physical (wave) optics; Geometric (ray) optics.
options.D: Geometric (ray) optics for all three tasks.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Three short real-world scenarios require ray, wave, or quantum models; options permute models to test correct matching.
concepts_tested: Modeling frameworks in optics (geometric vs physical)
Wave-particle duality and quantum optics (photons for photoelectric effect)
Historical and theoretical progression from ray to wave to quantum models

original_question.question: Why does the revelation principle justify focusing analysis on mechanisms where agents truthfully report their private information when trying to implement a desired outcome?

options.A: Because truth-telling is always a dominant strategy in any mechanism.
options.B: Because for any implementable outcome there exists a direct mechanism with truthful reporting that realizes the same outcome, so analysis can be restricted to truthful direct mechanisms.
options.C: Because agents' private information cannot be elicited through any mechanism other than direct truth-telling.
options.D: Because truth-telling guarantees efficiency regardless of information structure or beliefs.

concepts_tested: Revelation principle and truth-telling as sufficient for analysis
Design problem as an inverse/ends-first approach (goal function given, mechanism unknown)
Private information and implementation (agents have private information; mechanisms aim to elicit truthful information to achieve predefined outcomes)
parent_concepts: Signaling, screening, and mechanisms to mitigate moral hazard/adverse selection through contract design
Auction-based allocation and proportional share scheduling as concrete mechanisms, including how bidding or shares influence resource distribution.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why does the revelation principle allow designers to only study mechanisms where agents truthfully report private information?

options.A: Because truth-telling is always the best choice in every mechanism.
options.B: Because any outcome a mechanism can achieve can also be achieved by a direct mechanism where honest reports give the same outcome.
options.C: Because there is no other way to learn agents' private information than asking them to tell the truth.
options.D: Because honest reports always make the result efficient no matter what agents believe.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Made the wording short and clear for middle school. Replaced technical terms with simple phrases and kept the key idea: revelation principle lets analysis focus on truthful direct mechanisms that produce the same outcomes.

pass_2_attempt.question: A school principal wants students to report their private homework effort honestly and builds rules so honesty gives the best result; what does the revelation principle let the principal assume when designing those rules?

options.A: Students will report truthfully if the rules make honesty their best choice.
options.B: The principal must guess every possible lie before making rules.
options.C: The principal can force students to tell the truth without rewards.
options.D: Private information is useless so any rule will work.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Used a concrete school scenario to link private information, reverse design goal-first approach, and the revelation principle allowing focus on truthful-reporting mechanisms.
concepts_tested: Revelation principle (truthful reporting)
Mechanism design as designing rules from desired outcome
Private information and incentive-compatible design

original_question.question: Why is mediation among the state, market, and local community essential in spatial planning, and how does spatial planning typically realize this mediation?

options.A: By prioritizing market efficiency through unrestricted development rights.
options.B: By functioning as a boundary-spanning governance process that integrates sectoral policies, engages stakeholders, and coordinates development objectives to balance conflicting demands.
options.C: By leaving land-use decisions to local communities without state oversight, to reflect local preferences.
options.D: By enforcing a one-size-fits-all zoning code across regions to simplify planning.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Spatial planning mediates between competing demands on land from the state, market, and local community.
Concept 2: The three mechanisms of transformative spatial planning: involving stakeholders, integrating sectoral policies, and promoting development projects.
Concept 3: Planning systems are context-dependent and co-evolve with governance structures, leading to regional/time variations in planning form and impact.
parent_concepts: Concept 1: Regional planning uses macro-scale zoning and policy to integrate land use, housing, infrastructure, and environmental protection for sustainable regional growth.
Concept 2: Planning transportation corridors with hubs and spokes shapes accessibility, settlement roles, and future infrastructure needs, guiding regional development.
Concept 2

pass_1_attempt.question: Why must spatial planning mediate between the state, market and local communities, and how does it usually do this?

options.A: By focusing only on market efficiency and allowing unlimited development.
options.B: By acting across boundaries to balance demands, integrating sector policies, engaging stakeholders, and coordinating projects.
options.C: By leaving land-use choices only to local communities, without state coordination.
options.D: By using the same strict zoning rules everywhere to simplify planning.

Which option best balances the state's goals, the company's profit, and residents' wishes by using stakeholder meetings, integrating transport/environment/housing policies, and promoting projects while allowing changes when regional rules differ?

options.A: Hold public meetings with residents and the company, align transport, housing and environment rules, and fund both park and factory; adapt the plan if regional law differs.
options.B: Fast-track the factory with a single zoning rule to boost jobs and let the market decide residents' needs.
options.C: Change transport and housing policies to fit the factory and homes, but skip public meetings and delay the park project.
options.D: Ask residents and the company what they want and start both projects, but keep sector rules unchanged and keep the same plan across regions.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a scenario requiring students to identify all three planning mechanisms, balancing state/market/community, and recognizing that plans change with governance differences.
concepts_tested: Mediating competing demands of state, market, and local community
Three mechanisms: stakeholder involvement, policy integration, and promoting projects
Context-dependence of planning systems and changes across regions/time

original_question.question: Why do emergent properties arise in complex systems from the interactions of components, and what does this imply for explaining the system's behavior?

options.A: Because the global pattern is just a simple sum of individual component properties, enabling straightforward reduction to parts.
options.B: Because nonlinear interactions among components generate new organization and constraints at the system level, so the system's behavior cannot be fully explained by looking at components in isolation.
options.C: Because the properties of the system come entirely from the environment; the component rules are irrelevant.
options.D: Because once you know the initial conditions, the emergent property is predetermined and can be predicted by applying the component rules sequentially.

concepts_tested: Emergent properties: system-level features that are not properties of any individual component but arise from interactions among components.

Weak vs. strong emergence: weak emergence involves properties that are simulatable or analyzable after the fact and where components retain independence; strong emergence involves properties not reducible to or fully explainable by the components.

Role of emergence in complex systems and hierarchical levels: emergence explains life and other phenomena as properties of wholes within integrative levels, linking micro-level components to macro-level behaviors.
parent_concepts: Emergence and dynamic interactions: Complex properties of cells/organisms arise from interactions among genes, proteins, and metabolites within networks, requiring a holistic view rather than isolated components.
Emergent order and statistical patterns across scales: How and why do ecological systems exhibit order and recurring patterns despite local complexity?
Emergent properties: How interactions among neurons and glia lead to higher-level properties of neural circuits that cannot be inferred from single components alone.
Concept 1: Reductionism vs. autonomy in biology — whether biological phenomena can (or should) be fully reduced to chemistry/physics and what that implies for scientific explanations.

Concept 3: Self-similarity across scales and emergent/descriptive layers (how degrees of freedom and effective descriptions can change with scale, including the idea of dressed vs bare quantities like electric charge in QED).

pass_1_attempt.question: Why do new system-level properties appear when parts interact, and what does this mean for explaining the system's behavior?

options.A: Because the whole is just the sum of parts, so it can be fully explained by reducing to components.
options.B: Because parts interact in nonlinear ways to make new structure and constraints, so the whole cannot be explained by parts alone.
options.C: Because the system's properties come only from the environment, not from its parts.
options.D: Because knowing the initial conditions lets you predict the whole by applying component rules step by step.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Made the sentence shorter and clearer for middle school. Kept core idea that interactions cause new system-level properties and that parts alone cannot fully explain the whole. Options shortened to simple statements.

pass_2_attempt.question: A flock of birds that follows simple rules was copied by a computer model, while a mind causing actions seems not reducible to brain parts; which labels fit these examples?

options.A: Flock = weak emergence; Mind = strong emergence
options.B: Flock = strong emergence; Mind = weak emergence
options.C: Both are weak emergence
options.D: Neither is an emergent phenomenon

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Provided a concrete two-part scenario (birds vs mind) and asked students to match each to weak or strong emergence, testing simulation reducibility and irreducibility.
concepts_tested: emergent properties
weak vs strong emergence
complex systems and hierarchical levels

original_question.question: In communication studies, why does an unchanged persuasive message sometimes yield different behavioral outcomes across communities with different social structures?

options.A: Individuals' preexisting attitudes and dispositions alone determine responses, making social structure irrelevant.
options.B: Structural factors like norms, institutions, and available interpretive repertoires shape how a message is interpreted and acted upon, so outcomes vary with structure even if the message content is the same.
options.C: The channel medium alone determines outcomes; content plays no role.
options.D: Random chance governs outcomes, independent of both structural factors and individual agency.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Communication as a process that enables action (persuasion, information exchange, emotional expression) through appropriate media across cultures.
Concept 2: The interaction between social structure and individual agency in shaping communication patterns and outcomes.
Concept 3: The plurality and evolution of research methods and theoretical frameworks in communication studies (from qualitative/interpretive to computational approaches).
parent_concepts: Concept 2

pass_1_attempt.question: Why can the same persuasive message lead to different actions in different communities?

options.A: People's own attitudes alone decide their response, so social structure does not matter.
options.B: Social structures like norms, institutions, and shared ways of understanding change how people interpret and act on the message.
options.C: Only the channel (like TV or phone) decides the outcome; the message content and social structure do not matter.
options.D: Results happen by random chance and are not shaped by people or social structure.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Made the question short and clear; replaced jargon with simple phrases (e.g., 'shared ways of understanding'); kept the main idea that social structure shapes interpretation and action.

pass_2_attempt.question: A city uses TV, community meetings, and social media for a vaccine campaign while researchers interview people and analyze posts. Which choice best explains how communication, social structure, and research methods work together?

options.A: Different media let people share facts and feelings; social roles affect who listens; researchers use interviews and data analysis.
options.B: Only TV can persuade everyone, and social structure doesn't change responses.
options.C: Researchers must use only surveys because interviews are unreliable and theory isn't needed.
options.D: People act the same in all neighborhoods, so one method predicts outcomes perfectly.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a concrete public-health scenario; options contrast pluralistic, structural, and methodological views to test all three concepts.
concepts_tested: Communication enables persuasion, information exchange, and emotional expression via media
Social structure and individual agency shape who listens and how messages work
Plurality and evolution of research methods (qualitative interviews and quantitative data analysis)

original_question.question: How do the distinct but interconnected subfields of anthropology collectively explain a complex social phenomenon like ritual gift-giving?

options.A: By treating each subfield as examining the same phenomenon independently, so you can pick whichever provides the easiest explanation.
options.B: By obscuring differences; the subfields actually focus on different species, so their findings cannot be integrated.
options.C: By combining analysis of behavior and social meaning (sociocultural), language in social life (linguistic), human biology/evolution (biological), and material remnants through time (archaeology) to show how practices arise from and shape culture, communication, biology, and history.
options.D: By limiting explanation to archaeology’s study of past remains, since ritual gift-giving is a past activity and other subfields are irrelevant.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Anthropology as an interdisciplinary, integrative study of humanity across biological, cultural, linguistic, and archaeological dimensions.

Concept 2: Distinct yet interconnected subfields (social/cultural/linguistic anthropology; biological/physical anthropology; archaeology) each with specific focal questions (behavior, cultural meaning, language in social life, biology/evolution) that together explain human phenomena.

Concept 3: Archaeology as the study of past human activity through physical remains and its varying regional status within anthropology (e.g., its classification as a branch or independent field) highlighting relationships between method, time, and disciplinary boundaries.
parent_concepts: Concept 1: Culture is learned and transmitted through social learning (enculturation and socialization), which explains why cultures differ across societies.
Concept 2: Cultural norms function as guidelines that codify acceptable conduct and shape behavior and social expectations, with implications for group cohesion and the risks of monoculture.
Concept 1: Mind and culture are inseparable and mutually constitutive; people are shaped by culture, and culture is shaped by its people.
Concept 3: Cultural traditions and social practices regulate, express, and transform the human psyche, leading to diversity in mind, self, and emotion across cultures.
Folklore artifacts span verbal, material, and customary lore, forming an integrated expressive culture.
The field studies the etymology, history, and use of names, linking linguistic origin to social meaning.

Concept 2: The formation and inference of communities are influenced by spatial proximity and material culture, i.e., physical distance conditions social interaction and similarities in artifacts help reconstruct communities.
Concept 1: Objects mediate social relationships and communication across time and space; material culture is about the relationships between people and their things.
Concept 1: Language as a social practice and the ethnography of communication (language use within social situations and communities)

pass_1_attempt.question: How can different anthropology subfields together explain a ritual like gift-giving?

options.A: By each field studying it alone so you pick the easiest explanation.
options.B: By hiding differences; the fields study different things and cannot be joined.
options.C: By combining sociocultural (behavior and meaning), linguistic (language in social life), biological (human biology and evolution), and archaeology (material remains and history).
options.D: By using only archaeology and ignoring other fields, because gift rituals are from the past.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened the question to one sentence; made options brief and simple; explained subfield roles in option C using short phrases.

pass_2_attempt.question: A team studies pottery sherds, human bones, and recorded songs to learn about a past community; which statement best describes the fields and archaeology's role?

options.A: This is anthropology: it brings together archaeology, biological, and linguistic study; archaeology studies physical remains and is sometimes an independent field in Europe.
options.B: This is only archaeology because pottery and bones are ancient; songs are unrelated to studying the past.
options.C: Only biological study matters because bones tell the whole story; culture and language add no useful data.
options.D: Linguistics studies the songs and archaeology studies modern behavior; they are not part of the same discipline.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a short scenario requiring students to link multiple subfields and note archaeology's role and regional classification.
concepts_tested: Anthropology as interdisciplinary (biological, cultural, linguistic, archaeological)
Distinct subfields and their focal questions (bones → biology, songs → language, pottery → archaeology)
Archaeology studies physical remains and may be a branch of anthropology or an independent field regionally

original_question.question: Why is a multiscale, systems perspective essential in biophysics for connecting molecular interactions to cellular behavior, and how is this perspective typically implemented in research practice?

options.A: Because cellular outcomes can be predicted directly from a single-molecule rate constant without considering other components.
options.B: Because cellular behavior often arises from nonlinear interactions, feedback, and spatial organization that couple processes across molecular, cellular, and higher scales; this is implemented by building integrated multiscale models that merge molecular data with cellular and tissue-level constraints and by validating with cross-scale experiments.
options.C: Because molecular data alone suffices to predict cellular-level phenomena through straightforward extrapolation.
options.D: Because network context is irrelevant; outcomes depend only on a fixed interaction at the molecular level.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Biophysics applies physical theories (e.g., statistical mechanics, thermodynamics, chemical kinetics) to explain biomolecular interactions and phenomena.
Concept 2: Biophysics adopts a multiscale, systems perspective, connecting molecular interactions to cellular, organismal, and population-level biology.
Concept 3: Empirical observation and manipulation using diverse experimental techniques provide structure, dynamics, and interaction data that are integrated into theoretical models.
parent_concepts: Concept 2: Mechanical forces and cell movements are integral to morphogenesis and operate in concert with genetic programs to shape tissues.
Concept 3: Comparative biomechanics and locomotion energetics (how environment and mechanical factors—friction, drag, inertia, gravity—shape movement strategies and energy costs).
Concept 1: Structure–function relationships of biological macromolecules (proteins, nucleic acids, carbohydrates, lipids) underpin cellular processes.
Protein folding is driven by non-covalent interactions (hydrogen bonds, ionic interactions, Van der Waals forces, hydrophobic effects) and peptide-bond formation underpins polymerization.
Concept 1: Structure-function relationship — biomolecular architecture enables function, and changes in structure affect function; higher-order (tertiary) structure depends on primary structure.
Concept 3: Integration of experiment and computation — empirical structure determination paired with computational techniques (e.g., molecular dynamics) to study structure, dynamics, and function.
Concept 1: Mechanotransduction — the molecular mechanisms by which cells sense mechanical signals and convert them into biological responses.
Concept 1: Sliding filament mechanism – actin and myosin interactions and cross-bridge cycling drive contraction and sarcomere shortening.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why is a multiscale systems view needed to link molecular interactions to cell behavior, and how do scientists study this?

options.A: Because one molecule's rate constant can directly predict a cell's outcome, so no other parts matter.
options.B: Because cell behavior comes from complex, nonlinear interactions, feedbacks, and spatial organization across scales. Scientists build multiscale models combining molecular data with cellular and tissue constraints and test these with cross-scale experiments.
options.C: Because molecular data alone is enough to predict whole-cell behavior by simple extrapolation.
options.D: Because the network around a molecule does not matter; only a fixed molecular interaction decides outcomes.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened wording to one clear question. Used simple terms (complex interactions, models, experiments). Broke the answer into short sentences and kept the key idea: nonlinear cross-scale coupling and multiscale models validated by experiments.

pass_2_attempt.question: A scientist measures a protein with optical tweezers and microscopy, uses thermodynamics and statistical mechanics to predict cell signals. Which choice best describes the biophysics approach?

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a concrete lab scenario showing measurement tools and physical theories, then asked which option matches biophysics' experimental, theoretical, and multiscale approach.
concepts_tested: Applying physical theories (thermodynamics, statistical mechanics) to biomolecular interactions
Linking molecular measurements to cellular-level behavior (multiscale systems perspective)
Combining experimental techniques and theoretical models to explain biological phenomena

original_question.question: In political ecology, how does an environmental change interact with political and economic power to create a feedback loop that shapes future environmental outcomes?

options.A: Environmental change uniformly reduces production costs, leading to identical policy responses by all actors.
options.B: Environmental stress alters the relative dependence and bargaining power among different groups, prompting reforms in institutions and governance; these reforms then change who bears costs and benefits in subsequent ecological changes, creating a feedback loop.
options.C: The political and economic status quo determines environmental outcomes, but ecological changes do not feed back into power structures.
options.D: Changes in the environment are always accommodated by pre-existing institutions, with no need for new power dynamics.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Uneven distribution of environmental costs and benefits due to political, social, and economic differences across groups.
Concept 2: Environmental changes influence the political and economic status quo, creating feedback between ecological conditions and power/institutional arrangements.
Concept 3: The politicization of environmental issues through discourse, institutions, and social movements, shaping environmental outcomes.
parent_concepts: Concept 2: Embedding the environment in all levels of decision-making and viewing economic/political life as part of the ecological system.
Concept 3: Governance/mismanagement as a driver of degradation and related conflict, and the idea that the environment’s capacity to meet social/ecological needs shapes outcomes.
Socio-political and environmental impacts of resource extraction (how scarcity and governance influence conflict, human rights, and environmental damage)

Concept 3: Governance and social dimensions, including criticisms of fortress conservation and impacts on Indigenous peoples and local communities, affect the legitimacy and effectiveness of protected areas.
Concept 2: The goal of restoration can be historic-baseline vs. function-based, and goals are shaped by political, cultural, and contextual factors.
Concept 3: Economics and governance as integral drivers in agroecology, distinguishing it from natural ecology and shaping agricultural practices and outcomes.

pass_1_attempt.question: How can an environmental change interact with political and economic power to make a cycle that shapes future environments?

options.A: Environmental change always lowers production costs, so everyone makes the same policy choices.

options.C: Politics alone decides environmental outcomes, and ecological changes never change power structures.
options.D: Institutions always adapt, so environmental changes don't need new power or governance shifts.

pass_2_attempt.question: A factory pollutes a coastal town's fish, owners profit while fishermen lose income and protest—what outcome best shows political ecology?

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Made a concrete coastal factory scenario; options show different responses so students pick the one linking unequal impacts, political change, and social movement.
concepts_tested: Uneven distribution of environmental costs and benefits
Environmental change influencing political and economic status quo
Politicization of environmental issues via social movements and institutions

original_question.question: Why does a policy designed for subjective decision-making tend to be harder to test objectively than an operational policy?

options.A: Subjective policies rely on value judgments and trade-offs among multiple factors, making outcomes depend on preferences that are not fully observable or measurable; objective policies codify explicit rules and measurable criteria, enabling outcomes to be tested against observable inputs and results.
options.B: Subjective policies are never implemented, while objective policies always eliminate discretion, so only objective policies can be tested.
options.C: All policies are equally easy to test; the only difference is the quality of data available, not the decision framework itself.
options.D: Subjective policies focus on individual ethics and thus cannot influence organizational performance, whereas objective policies govern operations and can be empirically evaluated.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Policy as a deliberate system of guidelines and procedures intended to guide decision-making toward rational, desired outcomes, distinct from laws or rules that compel behavior.

Concept 2: The distinction between subjective versus objective decision-making in policy, and how these differences affect measurability and testing (e.g., subjective policies vs. objectively testable operational policies).
Concept 3: Evidence-based policy, including the three conditions for justification (comparative evidence, alignment with preferences, and a sound account of evidence and preferences).
parent_concepts: Concept 1
Concept 3

original_question.question: How do encryption and anonymity tools address different privacy invasion vectors in digital communication, and why can't encryption alone suffice?

options.A: Encryption shields both content and metadata from all observers, so anonymity is unnecessary.
options.B: Anonymity ensures the message cannot be decrypted, while encryption conceals who sent it, so they're functionally redundant.

original_question.question: How does weak foundationalism address the regress problem of justification, and what roles do fallible basic beliefs and non-deductive inferences play in this account?

options.A: It preserves infallible, deductively grounded basic beliefs that justify all others, thereby eliminating regress.
