options.B: Because other states recognise it, Marinia has full sovereignty and no one may ever intervene.
options.C: Rebel control means Marinia is not a state; recognition by others no longer matters.
options.D: Only de facto control matters; international recognition and UN rules do not affect sovereignty.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a short scenario showing legal recognition vs actual control, then asked about implications for sovereignty and UN intervention (Security Council and R2P).
concepts_tested: four aspects of sovereignty (territory, population, authority, recognition)
de jure vs de facto sovereignty and legitimacy
international law limits: non-interference, UN Security Council powers and Responsibility to Protect (R2P)

original_question.question: In deliberative democracy, authentic deliberation is the source of legitimacy. Why is it necessary to counteract distortions from unequal political power during deliberation, and how does this influence the perceived legitimacy of the final decision?

options.A: Because unequal political power can bias which arguments are heard and taken seriously, authentic deliberation aims to give all participants an equal opportunity to contribute and justify reasons; legitimacy then rests on the quality and public justification of the reasoning, not merely on who has more resources or higher status.
options.B: Because louder, wealthier voices should dominate the discussion, as their arguments are more likely to be correct; legitimacy comes from the outcome favored by those voices.
options.C: Because deliberation should produce a universal consensus; legitimacy arises only if everyone agrees, regardless of the reasoning.
options.D: Because the decision should be made by experts to ensure rationality; unequal power among lay participants is irrelevant because expertise overrides deliberation.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Authentic deliberation as the source of legitimacy, and the need to avoid distortions from unequal political power
Concept 2: Mechanisms to enable deliberation (random selection of lay citizens, resource/time allocation, deliberative polls, and the interplay of consensus vs. majority rule)

Concept 3: Relationships to other democratic forms and varied implementations (how deliberative democracy interacts with representative/direct democracy, and the distinction between elitist and populist approaches)
parent_concepts: Concept 3: Participatory, humanistic policy approach (inclusive policy dialogue, social/economic justice, environmental responsibility) shaping legitimacy and outcomes of curriculum frameworks.
Concept 3: Means/Processes (information sharing → consultation → negotiation/conclusion → collective bargaining) and how each step contributes to policy outcomes and social dialogue success.
Concept 2: Enabling conditions and mechanisms (support, leadership, and a forum) that organize problem identification and collaborative policy-making.
Concept 3: Practical forms (Consensus Building and Collaborative Network) as mechanisms to align needs, strategies, resources, and to build social capital for policy implementation.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why must deliberative democracy prevent unequal political power from shaping debates? How does that help people accept the final decision as legitimate?

options.A: Unequal power can bias which arguments are heard. Equal chances let participants justify reasons, so legitimacy rests on public, good reasoning.
options.B: Louder, wealthier voices should dominate discussions because their arguments are more likely to be right; legitimacy comes from their favored outcome.
options.C: Deliberation must make everyone agree. Legitimacy only exists if every person accepts the decision, no matter the reasons.
options.D: Experts should decide to ensure rationality. Unequal power among ordinary participants does not matter because experts override deliberation.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Split the original complex sentence into two short questions. Used simpler words and shorter option sentences while keeping the main idea that unequal power biases debate and that legitimacy comes from public, reasoned justification.

pass_2_attempt.question: Which option best describes deliberative democracy when 100 randomly chosen citizens study facts for a week, discuss as equals, then make a binding choice?

options.A: Randomly chosen citizens get balanced facts and time, discuss as equals, seek consensus, and their decision becomes law.
options.B: All city voters vote quickly by ballot with no group discussion; the majority winner decides.
options.C: Elected officials debate briefly while funded by interest groups, then the council votes on a law.
options.D: A small group of experts appointed by the mayor meets privately and sets the policy without public input.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a short civic scenario showing key features (random selection, info/time, equal discussion, consensus/majority, binding outcome) and contrasted it with non-deliberative options.
concepts_tested: Authentic deliberation as legitimacy and avoiding unequal power
Mechanisms: random selection, time/resources, deliberative discussion, consensus vs majority
Relations to other forms: contrast with representative, direct, and elite decision-making

original_question.question: How does adopting interdisciplinary methods from ecology and environmental history influence what counts as evidence in ecocritical analysis, and why does this strengthen understanding of a literary work's environmental context?

options.A: It broadens evidentiary sources to include ecological processes, historical environmental change, and power relations around resource use, allowing the text to be read as participating in real-world environmental dynamics rather than as isolated aesthetics.
options.B: It confines analysis to the text's description of nature as a timeless, pristine backdrop, treating ecological context as irrelevant.
options.C: It applies ecological quantitative models to measure the text's influence on readers, presuming reader numbers and growth rates determine interpretation.
options.D: It centers the author's explicit political agenda as the only source of ecological meaning, ignoring textual encoding and reception.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: The central relationship studied by ecocriticism is between literature and the physical environment, examined through an interdisciplinary lens.

Concept 2: Ecocriticism employs interdisciplinary methodologies by borrowing theories and methods from ecology, environmental history, biopolitics, etc., to analyze texts in their environmental contexts.

Concept 3: There is a normative dimension and scope expansion in ecocriticism, including aims to address environmental concerns and to recoup dignity for nature writing, as well as broadening analysis beyond traditional nature texts to diverse media and genres.
parent_concepts: Concept 1: The shift from monumental earthworks to practices that engage with ecological systems and social concerns (art as relational to environment and society).

pass_1_attempt.question: How do methods from ecology and environmental history change what counts as evidence in ecocriticism? Why does that help us see a text's environmental context more clearly?

options.A: They add evidence like ecological processes, past environmental change, and resource power, so the text links to real-world environmental issues.
options.B: They limit analysis to nature as a timeless, pristine backdrop, making ecology irrelevant.
options.C: They use ecological math to measure readers, wrongly assuming reader numbers decide meaning.
options.D: They treat the author's stated politics as the only ecological meaning and ignore the text and readers.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Split the original complex question into two short sentences, used simpler words, and made all options short and clear while keeping the original idea that interdisciplinary methods broaden evidence and deepen context.

pass_2_attempt.question: Maya reads a novel about a polluted city river and uses history, ecology, and poetry analysis to argue people must act. Which description best matches ecocriticism?

options.A: It studies how literature relates to the physical environment and borrows ideas from many fields to urge change.
options.B: It studies only nature poems and praises untouched wilderness, ignoring cities and politics.
options.C: It is a branch of ecology that tests rivers and plants in labs to get scientific data.
options.D: It treats literature purely as art and avoids moral or environmental questions.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a concrete scenario (novel about polluted river) to test the student’s understanding of ecocriticism as interdisciplinary study linking literature and environment with a normative aim; included plausible distractors.
concepts_tested: relationship between literature and the physical environment
use of interdisciplinary methods (ecology, history, etc.)
normative scope: addressing environmental concerns and broad media/genre coverage

original_question.question: How does multiplexing enable multiple concurrent communication sessions to share a single transmission medium, and why does this arrangement prevent interference between those sessions?

options.A: By allocating distinct, non-overlapping resources—such as time slots in time-division multiplexing, or frequency/wavelength bands in frequency- or wavelength-division multiplexing—to different sessions, so each session transmits in its own resource and signals stay separate.
options.B: By sending each session's data in a randomized order and relying on statistical separation at the receiver, which would not truly guarantee separation.
options.C: By stacking all sessions into the same continuous signal and using powerful error-correcting codes to separate them at the receiver, which would not prevent overlap.
options.D: By duplicating the medium for each session so that only one session uses the medium at a time, which defeats the purpose of sharing.

concepts_tested: Multiplexing as a mechanism to transmit several concurrent sessions over a single medium
Evolution of transmission media driven by physical limitations (from metal wires to optical fibre) enabling higher capacity and digital data
Internet as a media-independent, global network that reduces location and time constraints on communication
parent_concepts: Concept 1: Modulation encodes information by varying a carrier’s amplitude, frequency, or phase.
Concept 1: Spectrum management aims to maximize efficient use of the RF spectrum while mitigating interference and delivering social/economic benefits.

pass_1_attempt.question: How does multiplexing let many sessions share one transmission medium without interfering with each other?

options.A: By giving each session its own separate resource (time slot, frequency, or wavelength) so signals do not overlap.
options.B: By mixing data randomly and hoping the receiver can separate it statistically.
options.C: By combining all sessions into one signal and using error correction to recover each.
options.D: By giving each session its own separate medium so only one uses it at a time.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened to one clear sentence and simpler words; options made short and plausible. Kept the idea of separate time/frequency/wavelength resources preventing overlap.

pass_2_attempt.question: An island's single undersea cable must carry phone calls, TV and internet at the same time; which set of steps best explains how engineers solved this and let anyone access services worldwide?

options.A: Divide the cable into channels (multiplexing). Replace copper with optical fibre to raise capacity and use the Internet for global, anytime access.
options.B: Add many more copper wires. Send data by postal mail, then build radio towers for worldwide reach.
options.C: Use vacuum tubes to boost signals. Split time slots randomly and keep only local switching.
options.D: Send everything as analogue over one wire. Ignore noise and hope it still works.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created an island undersea cable scenario and asked for correct sequence: multiplexing, fibre replacing copper due to limits, and Internet enabling global, time-independent access.
concepts_tested: Multiplexing to share a single transmission medium
Evolution from metal wires to optical fibre due to physical limits and capacity needs
Internet as a media-independent network that reduces location and time constraints

original_question.question: Why does music theory focus on describing processes and general principles rather than analyzing a single composition, and how does this scope relate to the roles of practice and musicology?

options.A: It aims to capture cross-piece patterns that practitioners can apply, with practice translating those principles into performance or composition, while analysis centers on the details of a single work to illuminate its unique features.
options.B: It treats every piece as unique so theory cannot generalize; practice ignores theories; musicology collects only historical facts.
options.C: Theory and analysis are identical in focus; practice is separate and unaffected by theoretical descriptions.
options.D: Theory's scope includes only rudiments; practice uses those but musicology never studies broader principles.

concepts_tested: The relationship between theory, practice, and musicology (how theory describes processes and general principles and how it differs from analysis)
The scope and inclusivity of music theory (ranging from rudiments to tuning systems to silence, and how historical perspectives like the Quadrivium shape modern definitions)
Transmission and cross-cultural development of music theory (oral and written traditions, artifacts, and cross-cultural evidence shaping theory)
parent_concepts: Consonance/dissonance as both a perceptual/categorical concept and a historical/theoretical point, including ratios and physiological interpretations.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why does music theory describe general processes and principles instead of only studying one composition? How do these general ideas connect to musical practice and musicology?

options.A: It finds patterns across many works so musicians can use them; practice applies these ideas, while analysis studies one work's details.
options.B: It treats every piece as totally unique so theory cannot generalize; practice ignores theory; musicology only records history.
options.C: Theory and analysis are the same thing; practice is separate and does not use theory.
options.D: Theory only covers basic rules; practice uses them, and musicology never studies broader principles.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences, simpler vocabulary, removed technical examples, and split the original complex question into two clear sentences.

pass_2_attempt.question: A researcher studies key signatures, ancient clay flutes, and silence in performances; which choice best explains how music theory, practice, and musicology connect?

options.A: Music theory studies rules like notation and tuning and can include silence. As part of musicology it studies materials and general principles and spreads via instruments, oral teaching, and writings.
options.B: Music theory is only about practical skills like playing and never studies silence. Musicology is analysis of single works.
options.C: Music theory must be written down; oral traditions and instruments are not part of theory.
options.D: The Quadrivium proved music theory is only math and unrelated to tuning or instruments.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Used a researcher scenario linking notation, artifacts, and silence. Options contrast theory vs. practice, musicology vs. analysis, and transmission routes.
concepts_tested: relationship between theory practice and musicology
scope and inclusivity of music theory (notation, tuning, silence, historical views)
transmission and cross-cultural development of music theory (oral, written, artifacts)

original_question.question: How does compositionality explain deriving the meaning of a sentence like "Every student read a book" from the meanings of its parts and their arrangement?

options.A: The sentence's meaning is determined solely by the most informative word in isolation.
options.B: The meanings of the individual words are combined according to the grammatical structure, with operators like quantifiers applied to predicates built from the other words, to yield the sentence meaning.
options.C: The meaning arises from a holistic mental image of the entire sentence, without relying on the meanings of individual words.
options.D: The truth-conditions of the sentence are fixed by the surrounding discourse context, ignoring the internal word-by-word composition.

concepts_tested: Compositionality: how the meaning of a complex expression (e.g., a sentence) is built from the meanings of its parts and their arrangement.
Sense vs. Reference: the distinction between the ideas/concepts associated with an expression (sense) and the actual object it points to (reference).

Theoretical frameworks and interdisciplinarity: the variety of theories (referential, ideational, causal, truth-conditional, use, inferentialist) and how semantics interacts with disciplines like logic, computer science, and psychology.
parent_concepts: Concept 2: The distinction between vagueness and ambiguity and its implications for linguistic meaning and interpretation.

pass_1_attempt.question: How does compositionality explain the meaning of the sentence "Every student read a book"?

options.A: The sentence meaning comes only from the single most informative word.
options.B: Word meanings combine by grammar. Quantifiers like "every" apply to the others' meanings to make the sentence meaning.
options.C: Meaning comes from a whole mental image, not from the individual words.
options.D: The sentence's truth is fixed only by the conversation context, ignoring word composition.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened wording to one clear question sentence; options made short and age-appropriate; kept the idea that grammar combines word meanings and quantifiers form sentence meaning.

pass_2_attempt.question: Which scenario best shows compositionality, sense versus reference, and semantics used in logic or computing?

options.A: A logician writes 'the morning star is bright' in logic; the words combine for meaning, while 'morning star' and 'evening star' point to the same planet.
options.B: A programmer shows i += 1 and i = i + 1 have the same behavior, though written differently.
options.C: Someone learns the idiom 'kick the bucket' means 'die', so the whole phrase's meaning is not from its parts.
options.D: A historian notes 'Roger Bannister' and 'the first man to run a four-minute mile' name the same person, though descriptions differ.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Made four short scenarios; correct choice combines compositional sentence building, sense vs reference (morning/evening star), and use of logic/computation.
concepts_tested: Compositionality
Sense versus reference
Interdisciplinary use of semantics (logic/computer science)

original_question.question: Why is it conceptually necessary in historical analysis to distinguish background conditions from causal mechanisms when explaining why a historical event occurred?

options.A: Because background conditions are the same as the immediate trigger and identifying them gives the same explanation.
options.B: Because background conditions establish the structural context that makes outcomes possible, while causal mechanisms show the processes that translate those conditions into the specific outcome; together they explain multiple, interacting causes rather than a single trigger.
options.C: Because only the immediate trigger matters; considering background conditions and mechanisms would overcomplicate the explanation.
options.D: Because causal mechanisms are isolated; they function independently of background conditions.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Evidence-based reconstruction and source criticism as methods to explain past events
Concept 2: Causal explanations (background conditions and causal mechanisms) for why events occurred
Concept 3: Historiography and methodological pluralism (positivism, Annales, Marxism, postmodernism) shaping interpretation and narratives
parent_concepts: Concept 1: Public history aims to make historical knowledge useful and accessible to the general public, not solely to academics.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why do historians separate background conditions from causal mechanisms when explaining why an event happened?

options.A: Because background conditions are the same as the immediate trigger, so they give the same explanation.
options.B: Because background conditions set the wider context. Mechanisms show the processes that produce the event.
options.C: Because only the immediate trigger matters; background conditions and mechanisms just make explanations too complex.
options.D: Because causal mechanisms work alone and do not depend on background conditions.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Made the wording shorter and clearer, split the key idea into two short sentences, and kept the distinction between context (conditions) and process (mechanisms).

pass_2_attempt.question: A historian studies a 1950 factory explosion with reports, diaries, photos, and soil tests. Which plan best shows source criticism, causal explanation, and several historiographical approaches?

options.A: Verify dates and authors, compare sources, check soil tests, and explain economic pressures, worker conditions, and long-term trends.
options.B: Use only the official factory report and accept its conclusion without checking other evidence.
options.C: Trust oral stories alone and focus only on individuals' motives, ignoring material tests.
options.D: Form a theory first, then pick facts that fit and claim one universal law explains all accidents.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a concrete detective-style historical scenario and gave four research plans; A combines external/internal criticism, causal mechanisms, and multiple historiographical lenses.
concepts_tested: source criticism and evidence-based reconstruction
causal explanations and background conditions
historiography and methodological pluralism (different approaches to interpreting evidence)

original_question.question: Why does combining qualitative and quantitative techniques provide a more complete understanding of consumer needs, and how do their roles differ in informing strategy?

options.A: Qualitative methods uncover depth, context, and latent needs, generating hypotheses about motivations; quantitative methods measure how widespread those needs are across the population, enabling generalization and prioritization for decision making.
options.B: Qualitative methods alone can generalize findings to the entire market, while quantitative methods only collect a few anecdotes.
options.C: Quantitative methods are primarily for exploring new ideas, while qualitative methods are for confirming them with large samples.
options.D: Qualitative methods provide numerical estimates of market size, while quantitative methods reveal emotions in small groups.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Market research as a systematic, data-driven process that yields insights to support decision making and strategy.
Concept 2: The roles and differences of qualitative vs. quantitative techniques and how they contribute to understanding consumer needs.
Concept 3: Primary vs. secondary research and how each source fits into designing research to assess market size, needs, and competition.
parent_concepts: Concept 3: Segmentation results in segment profiles and the identification of high-yield or growing segments to guide resource allocation and strategy.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why use both qualitative and quantitative methods to study consumer needs, and how do they help choose strategy?

options.A: Qualitative methods find deep reasons and hidden needs and create ideas. Quantitative methods measure how common those needs are so firms can prioritize.
options.B: Qualitative methods alone can be applied to whole markets. Quantitative methods only collect a few stories.
options.C: Quantitative methods are mainly for exploring new ideas. Qualitative methods confirm them using large samples.
options.D: Qualitative methods give numbers for market size. Quantitative methods show emotions from small groups.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences and used simpler words. Kept the core idea that qualitative finds depth and hypotheses and quantitative measures scope and helps prioritise for strategy.

pass_2_attempt.question: A soda company has interview notes, past sales numbers, and online industry reports. Which research plan best uses systematic data, qualitative and quantitative methods, and primary and secondary sources to estimate market size and customer needs?

options.A: Combine interviews (qualitative primary), a large survey for numbers (quantitative primary), and industry reports (secondary) for market size.
options.B: Rely only on past sales numbers and ignore interviews and reports.
options.C: Use only interviews and focus groups to get exact market size and forecasts.
options.D: Run expensive ads and wait to see which flavor sells best without collecting data.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a concrete business scenario requiring multi-step reasoning about using qualitative vs quantitative methods and primary vs secondary sources for systematic decision-making.
concepts_tested: Market research as systematic, data-driven decision support
Roles of qualitative vs quantitative techniques
Primary vs secondary research for market size and needs

original_question.question: Why do ecological economists argue that natural capital cannot be freely substituted by human-made capital in sustaining long-run welfare, and how does this relate to the idea of strong sustainability?

options.A: Because many ecosystem services are non-substitutable or have irreversible losses; strong sustainability requires preserving critical natural capital stocks, so substitution by manufactured capital is limited and cannot fully compensate when thresholds are crossed.
options.B: Because human-made capital can always replicate natural services, so strong sustainability rejects any limits on substitution between types of capital.
options.C: Because market prices capture all values of nature, making substitutions always costless and optimal under strong sustainability.
options.D: Because natural capital and human-made capital are perfectly substitutable in all contexts, so strong sustainability places no special value on preserving natural capital.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: The economy as a subsystem of Earth's ecosystem and the primacy of natural capital (including strong vs. weak sustainability).
Concept 2: Time, intergenerational equity, irreversibility, and uncertainty as guiding principles for sustainable development and economic valuation.

Concept 3: The normative nature of ecological economics and critiques of standard methods (e.g., cost-benefit analysis) with alternative approaches (e.g., positional analysis) and connections to justice and care.
parent_concepts: Assessment/valuation mechanisms: natural capital asset checks link environmental changes to human well-being and economic outcomes (and the issue of unpriced natural capital).
Ecosystem services/natural capital as a framework for linking ecological processes to human outcomes and policy (how ecosystems provide value and sustain human societies)
Concept 3: Economics and governance as integral drivers in agroecology, distinguishing it from natural ecology and shaping agricultural practices and outcomes.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why do ecological economists say we cannot replace nature with man-made things for long-term well-being? How does this idea link to strong sustainability?

options.A: Many ecosystem services are non-substitutable or lost forever; strong sustainability says protect crucial natural capital.
options.B: Because man-made capital can always replicate nature, so strong sustainability allows substituting natural capital freely.
options.C: Because market prices capture all of nature's value, substitution is costless and preferred under strong sustainability.
options.D: Because natural and human-made capital are perfectly interchangeable, strong sustainability gives no special duty to conserve nature.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Split the original complex sentence into two short, clear sentences. Used simpler words and kept options brief and plausible while keeping the original answer A unchanged.

pass_2_attempt.question: A town must decide whether to allow mining in a rare forest that provides medicines and cleans water. Which choice best follows ecological economics, which treats the economy as part of nature, values natural capital as often irreplaceable, and cares about future generations and justice?

options.A: Protect the forest now, reject mining, and use positional multi-criteria analysis to weight future risks and justice.
options.B: Accept the mining payment and spend it on new factories and tech that can replace the forest later.
options.C: Sell the mine but pay for carbon credits and plant some trees to show offsetting.
options.D: Leave the decision to a simple cost–benefit test that counts only immediate money and jobs.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a town/forest scenario requiring trade-offs to test strong vs weak sustainability, intergenerational equity, irreversibility, and normative critique of cost–benefit analysis; correct option emphasizes protection, time, justice, and positional/multi-criteria analysis.
concepts_tested: Economy as a subsystem and primacy of natural capital (strong vs weak sustainability)
Intergenerational equity, irreversibility, and uncertainty in long-term valuation
Normative critique of cost–benefit analysis and use of positional/multi-criteria approaches to include justice and care

original_question.question: In systems ecology, why does applying energy flow as the organizing principle enable cross-ecosystem comparisons of function, and how does this connect to emergent properties?

options.A: Because energy constraints are identical for all ecosystems, so all systems behave the same at every scale, making emergent properties simply the sum of components.
options.B: Because energy throughput imposes scale-spanning constraints on production, transfer, and losses, causing similar network-wide patterns to arise from different component identities; emergent properties then reflect system-level constraints rather than specific species.
options.C: Because focusing on energy flow eliminates the need to model exchanges with the environment, allowing all systems to be treated as closed and making emergent properties merely the internal processing of matter.
options.D: Because energy-based diagrams predict exact species-level outcomes from energy inputs alone, enabling precise forecasts of community composition without empirical data.

concepts_tested: Emergent properties and holism: ecosystems function as complex systems where system-level behavior arises from interactions among components.
Energetics as organizing principle: energy flow and energetics unify the analysis across scales; the Energy Systems Language is used to diagram and analyze flows.
Open systems and boundaries: ecosystems are open systems; the choice of boundaries and exchanges with the environment shapes analysis and understanding.
parent_concepts: Concept 2: Interdependence and cross-ecosystem relationships, including nebulous boundaries and interactions with adjacent ecosystems that influence biodiversity and productivity

pass_1_attempt.question: Why does studying ecosystems by their energy flow let scientists compare very different ecosystems? How does this link to emergent properties?

options.A: Because energy rules are identical everywhere, so ecosystems behave the same and emergent properties are just sums.
options.B: Because energy flow sets limits across scales for production, transfer, and loss, it makes similar network patterns appear. Emergent properties then reflect system-level limits rather than individual species.
options.C: Because focusing on energy ignores exchanges with the environment, so systems are treated as closed and emergent properties are internal.
options.D: Because energy diagrams predict exact species outcomes from inputs, they let scientists forecast communities without data.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened sentences, removed technical jargon, and split ideas into simple lines while keeping the idea that energy constraints cause similar system-level patterns and emergent properties.

pass_2_attempt.question: A scientist studies a pond with fish, algae, sunlight, and fertilizer runoff; which action best follows systems ecology?

options.A: Draw the pond boundary that includes sunlight and runoff, and show energy/material flows causing an algal bloom.
options.B: Study only the fish species to predict pond health without showing energy flows.
options.C: Treat the pond as a closed system and ignore fertilizer and sunlight inputs.
options.D: Measure only the money cost to remove algae and ignore ecological flows.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Used a concrete pond scenario to require students to pick the holistic, energetics-focused, open-systems approach; distractors reflect partial or incorrect approaches.
concepts_tested: emergent properties and holism
energetics and energy/material flows
open systems and boundary choices

original_question.question: How do formal (external sanctions) and informal (internalized norms) social control mechanisms interact to sustain social order, and why is each necessary for the other's effectiveness?

options.A: They operate independently; the presence of one makes the other redundant, so order is maintained by either norms or sanctions alone.
options.B: Internalization completely eliminates the need for sanctions, so formal controls are rarely exercised.
options.C: External sanctions reinforce and legitimate internalized norms, while internalization reduces the reliance on sanctions by guiding behavior in the absence of enforcement; together they cover gaps and prevent disorder.
options.D: Formal sanctions generate norms from scratch, making informal control unnecessary in modern society.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Formal vs informal social control as complementary mechanisms that regulate behavior (internalization through socialization vs external sanctions by government) to maintain social order.
Concept 2: The relationship between social control and deviance/social order (including the paradox of deviance and Durkheim’s perspective on how control sustains adherence to laws).

Concept 3: Historical/theoretical evolution of social control (key thinkers and shifts in meaning from regulation of society to conformity, and the role of religion and psychotherapy as forms of social control).
parent_concepts: Concept 1: Punishment serves multiple, interrelated purposes (retribution, deterrence, rehabilitation, incapacitation) and these goals influence how and why punishment is applied.

pass_1_attempt.question: How do formal rules (laws and punishments) and informal rules (personal values learned in society) work together to keep order?

options.A: They work separately; either laws or values alone keep order, so one makes the other unnecessary.
options.B: If people learn the values, no punishments are needed, so laws are rarely used.
options.C: Laws and punishments back up the values people learn. Those values cut the need for punishments, and together they prevent disorder.
options.D: Laws create values by themselves, so personal values are not needed in modern societies.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened language, defined formal vs informal in parentheses, split complex idea into short sentences while keeping the original relationship.

pass_2_attempt.question: A town uses family lessons, church sermons, therapy, and police laws to keep people from breaking rules; what idea best explains how these methods work together?

options.A: Informal lessons build inner values, formal laws punish, and historically control shifted toward making individuals conform.
options.B: Only police laws matter; family and therapy do not change behavior.
options.C: Social control always meant only state power, never personal beliefs or therapy.
options.D: Deviance is good and cannot be stopped by rules or values.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a short town scenario listing informal (family, church, therapy) and formal (police laws) controls; options test complementarity, effect on deviance, and historical shift toward conformity.
concepts_tested: Formal vs informal social control and how they work together
Relationship between social control and deviance / Durkheim's idea that control sustains law-abiding behavior
Historical/theoretical evolution of social control (shift toward individual conformity; roles of religion and therapy)

original_question.question: Why does religious pluralism, beyond mere absence of persecution, matter for achieving equality and inclusion in a diverse society?

options.A: Toleration already guarantees equal civil rights and public participation for all groups, so pluralism only adds symbolic recognition.
options.B: Toleration can permit ongoing unequal treatment in public life, whereas pluralism requires institutions to guarantee equal rights, fair representation, and a shared public square open to diverse beliefs.
options.C: Pluralism demands that all religions adopt the same truth claims, which is necessary to avoid conflicts and ensure unity.
options.D: Pluralism treats religion as a private matter and prohibits any public involvement by religious groups.

concepts_tested: Distinction between religious toleration and religious pluralism, and the idea that pluralism implies more than the absence of persecution (progressing toward equality and inclusion).
Forms of inclusivism and the epistemic stance that multiple religions may contain true or valuable elements, including debates over universal truths, tolerance, relativism, and syncretism.

Relationship between religion and the state, including secularism as neutrality and the concept of a friendly separation of church and state, plus related ideas like ecumenism and harmonious coexistence across religious groups.
parent_concepts: Concept 1: Ecumenism as a principle of unity and cooperation across Christian denominations, enabled by dialogue and shared goals.

pass_1_attempt.question: Why does religious pluralism matter more than mere toleration for fairness and inclusion in a diverse society?

options.A: Toleration already guarantees equal civil rights and public participation. So pluralism only adds symbolic recognition.
options.B: Toleration can allow unequal treatment in public life. Pluralism asks institutions to guarantee equal rights, fair representation, and a shared public square.
options.C: Pluralism demands all religions adopt the same truth claims to avoid conflict.
options.D: Pluralism treats religion as private and bans public involvement by religious groups.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Question and options were shortened and split into clear short sentences. Complex phrases like 'religious liberty' and legal examples were removed while keeping the toleration vs pluralism contrast.

pass_2_attempt.question: Which example best shows religious pluralism rather than mere toleration?

options.A: No punishments for different faiths, but minorities cannot hold public office
options.B: Government stays neutral, all faiths have equal rights, and groups cooperate
options.C: People blend beliefs from many religions into one new faith
options.D: Everyone says all religions are equally true with no differences

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Used a concrete town/country policy scenario to contrast toleration vs pluralism, and included options representing syncretism and relativism to test inclusivist forms and state neutrality.
concepts_tested: difference between toleration and pluralism (equality vs absence of persecution)
forms of inclusivism and related ideas (relativism, syncretism) and state neutrality/friendly separation (secularism/ecumenism)

original_question.question: How does immunization act as a mechanism-led preventive strategy to reduce disease incidence in a population, even when vaccination coverage is not 100%?

options.A: It cures infections in vaccinated individuals, stopping transmission.
options.B: It reduces the exposure of the population to pathogens by changing environmental factors.
options.C: It reduces the number of susceptible individuals, lowering the effective reproduction number and creating herd immunity that protects unvaccinated people.
options.D: It identifies and isolates infected individuals early to prevent spread.

concepts_tested: The prevention framework: primal/primordial, primary, secondary, and tertiary prevention and how interventions align with stages of disease.
Interaction of risk factors and preventive action: environmental, genetic, disease agents, and lifestyle influence disease development, which anticipatory actions seek to modify.
Screening and immunization as mechanism-led prevention: how identifying risk factors and preventing disease onset/progression demonstrates cause-effect in preventive care.
parent_concepts: Concept 1: The purpose and trade-offs of screening (early detection and potential harms such as overdiagnosis and false security; reliance on test performance and disease natural history).

pass_1_attempt.question: How can vaccines lower disease spread in a community even if not everyone gets vaccinated?

options.A: It cures infections in vaccinated people, so they cannot spread disease.
options.B: It changes the environment so people are not exposed to germs.
options.C: It cuts the number of people who can get sick, lowering spread and protecting unvaccinated people.
options.D: It finds and isolates sick people early to stop disease spread.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened wording, used one clear question sentence, and made each option a brief plausible idea while keeping the herd immunity concept.

pass_2_attempt.question: Put these actions in order from prevention that acts earliest in life to the one that helps after disease causes disability: A) advice/support during pregnancy and baby's first year, B) vaccinating children, C) blood pressure checks to find early disease, D) physical therapy after a stroke.

options.A: Advice/support during pregnancy and baby's first year → Vaccinate children → Blood pressure checks → Physical therapy after stroke
options.B: Vaccinate children → Advice/support during pregnancy and baby's first year → Blood pressure checks → Physical therapy after stroke
options.C: Advice/support during pregnancy and baby's first year → Blood pressure checks → Vaccinate children → Physical therapy after stroke
options.D: Blood pressure checks → Vaccinate children → Advice/support during pregnancy and baby's first year → Physical therapy after stroke

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a concrete sequence of four real interventions and asked students to order them from earliest (primal/primordial) to latest (tertiary), testing mapping of examples to prevention levels.
concepts_tested: prevention framework levels (primal/primordial, primary, secondary, tertiary)
screening and immunization as mechanisms and timing of preventive actions

original_question.question: How does the coupling between climate (orographic precipitation) and surface processes influence the evolution of mountain height, and why can this create a self-limiting feedback?

options.A: Orographic precipitation increases chemical weathering that thickens soils, deepening the base of valleys and making mountains taller over time.
options.B: Orographic precipitation on windward slopes increases runoff and erosion, lowering peak elevations; as height declines, the orographic effect weakens, reducing rainfall and erosion rates, producing a negative feedback toward a steady state.
options.C: Higher rainfall strengthens rock strength through hydration, reducing erosion and allowing mountains to maintain their height indefinitely.
options.D: Climate acts independently of geomorphic processes, so precipitation does not feed back to alter mountain height.

concepts_tested: Concept 1: Landscape evolution arises from a balance between additive processes (uplift, deposition) and subtractive processes (erosion, subsidence).

Concept 2: Landforms result from the coupling of surface processes (water, wind, ice, biology, chemical weathering) with geologic processes (uplift, volcanism, isostasy), i.e., landscapes as a system at the intersection of lithosphere, atmosphere, hydrosphere, and biosphere.
Concept 3: Climate–geomorphology feedbacks influence topography and its evolution (e.g., orographic precipitation altering hydrologic regimes, which in turn affect erosion and deposition).
parent_concepts: Concept 1: Transport mechanisms and their controlling factors (gravity vs. fluid motion; particle size; fluid density/viscosity) and how these enable sediment movement in different media (air, water, ice).

pass_1_attempt.question: How can orographic (mountain-caused) precipitation and erosion change mountain height, and why does this make a self-limiting feedback?

options.A: Orographic rain increases chemical weathering and soil thickness, deepening valleys and raising mountains.
options.B: Orographic rain on windward slopes boosts runoff and erosion, lowering peaks; lower height reduces orographic rain, slowing erosion.
options.C: More rain hydrates rocks and makes them stronger, reducing erosion so mountains keep their height.
options.D: Climate acts separately from surface processes, so precipitation does not change mountain height.

pass_1_attempt.simplification_notes: Shortened wording, explained 'orographic', kept the core idea of rainfall-driven erosion reducing height and weakening the orographic effect.

pass_2_attempt.question: A mountain range slowly uplifts while moist winds cause heavy rain on its windward side, and rivers carry the extra sediment to a nearby plain; how will the land most likely change over time?

options.A: Uplift always makes the mountains taller, regardless of erosion.
options.B: Rain increases erosion on the windward side; rivers deposit sediment on the plain and net change depends on rates.
options.C: Wind forms dunes on the peaks and causes the mountains to rise faster.
options.D: Climate does not affect topography; only tectonics shapes the land.

pass_2_attempt.generation_notes: Created a short scenario combining uplift, erosion/deposition, and climate feedback to test balance of additive/subtractive processes and coupling of surface and geologic forces.
concepts_tested: Balance of uplift/deposition versus erosion/subsidence
Coupling of surface processes (rain, rivers) with geologic uplift
Climate feedbacks (orographic rainfall increasing erosion and sediment transport)
